title: Plain-language writing guide + reading-level targets (+ attestation fallback)
context: Museum labels must be understood by general visitors including non-native speakers, low-literacy adults, children, and people with cognitive or reading disabilities. The project treats plain language as a measured gate (CEFR A2-B1 / configured reading-level threshold), not an aspiration. Drafting agents and human reviewers need a concrete guide to apply consistently across all source-language descriptions.
objective: Write a plain-language writing guide covering: the project's reading-level target (CEFR A2-B1 / Flesch-Kincaid-family threshold for English; reviewer attestation for languages without an automated metric); practical rules for short sentences, common vocabulary, defined jargon, active voice, and concrete description; the label scope (≈40-120 words, title/date/maker/materials/plain description); proper-noun and period-term handling; and the reviewer-attestation fallback wording for languages without automated readability metrics.
acceptanceCriteria: States the configured reading-level target for source-language descriptions (CEFR A2-B1 / Flesch-Kincaid-family threshold for English) with the rationale

Provides concrete writing rules: sentences ≤20 words on average, common vocabulary over specialist jargon, jargon defined in-line or via glossary reference, active voice preferred, concrete and specific over abstract
Defines the standard label scope: title, date, maker/origin, materials/technique, and a 40-120-word plain-language description of what the object is and why it matters for a general visitor
Specifies how proper nouns, period terms, and material names are treated (preserve, do not simplify, reference the glossary for transliteration/do-not-translate items)
Provides the reviewer-attestation fallback wording for languages without reliable automated readability metrics
Is referenced by the reviewer checklist (review-001) and the structural-check CI tooling (tooling-001)
output: guides/plain-language.md — a concise, self-contained guide for human and agent use when drafting source-language descriptions
